preFix.title: Latch hook geometric cushion cover
preFix.subtitle: A patterned cushion cover worked from a geometric grid design on a latch hook canvas, approximately 45 x 45 cm
preFix.excerpt: A latch hook cushion cover is worked by knotting short pre-cut lengths of yarn to a canvas mesh using a latch hook tool. Each knot fills one canvas square, building up a grid-based design. Geometric patterns (diamonds, chevrons, and stepped triangles) work particularly well because they align cleanly with the square mesh.
preFix.sourceNotes: Latch hook technique for cushion covers and rugs is consistent across UK craft instruction. The canvas mesh standard and knot method described are consistent across manufacturer guides. Observation-led.

content.text: holds one knot tied with the
tool, and each knot contributes one dot of colour to the pattern. The resulting

makes the finished piece soft to the touch and durable in use. Geometric designs suit this square-grid medium particularly well: a 45 x 45 cm cushion worked on standard 5-mesh canvas gives a working grid of approximately 90 x 90 squares.

items.name: Latch hook canvas, 5-mesh, cut to 50 x 50 cm (5 cm margin on each side)
Pre-cut latch hook yarn packs in 3 to 5 colours for the geometric design
Graph paper or printed grid for charting the geometric pattern
Backing fabric: felt or cotton canvas, 50 x 50 cm

content.text: On graph paper, chart a 90 x 90 square grid and colour in the geometric design using pencil or coloured pens. Diamonds, chevrons, and stepped triangles all divide cleanly on a square grid. Count the total number of squares for each colour and calculate the yarn quantity: standard pre-cut packs contain approximately 320 cuts per pack; divide the square count by 320 to determine the number of packs per colour.
Step 2: Work the latch hook knots

Work from the bottom of the canvas upward to prevent completed pile from obscuring the working area. To tie each knot: fold a pre-cut yarn length over the hook above the latch; push the hook through one mesh square from front to back; catch both yarn tails with the open hook; draw the hook back through the square, which causes the latch to close and pull the yarn tails through the loop on the hook. Pull firmly to secure the knot. Follow the charted design one square at a time, checking the pattern chart after each row.

When all knots are complete, fold the canvas margin (the unworked border) to the back of the piece and stitch it flat. Cut the backing fabric to 46 x 46 cm. Place the backing right side against the pile, pin, and stitch three sides together with a 1 cm seam using a heavy needle and strong thread. Insert the cushion pad, fold in the seam allowance on the open side, and slip stitch closed. The pile on the front of the cushion will be slightly longer than the pre-cut standard; trim any uneven ends with scissors to level the pile surface.

items.fix: Fold the canvas in half in both directions and mark the centre square with a pen before starting. Chart the geometric design from the centre outward so both halves mirror each other. Begin knotting at the centre square.
items.cause: The pattern was not centred on the canvas before knotting began
items.problem: The geometric pattern does not align symmetrically and one side is wider than the other

items.fix: For loose knots already in the canvas, pull both tails firmly toward the back of the canvas and re-tighten. Future knots should be pulled until the base of the fold sits snugly against the canvas surface and does not move when tugged.
items.cause: The yarn tails were not pulled firmly enough through the loop when forming each knot
items.problem: Knots are pulling out or loose after the cover is complete
